questions.question: What should most directly drive the choice between SQL and a NoSQL store?
options: Which database is most popular this year
The system's access patterns — how data is actually read and written
The programming language of the application
Whichever has the shortest name
questions.explanation: Picking a database means picking a data model, and the data model should follow from access patterns: do you read by key, by range, with joins, or by graph traversal? 'SQL vs NoSQL' is the wrong framing — the access pattern narrows the field for you.

questions.question: Is 'NoSQL' a single kind of database?
options: Yes — all NoSQL databases work the same way
No — it lumps together key-value, document, wide-column, and graph models, each suited to different patterns
Yes — NoSQL just means 'no SQL language'
No — NoSQL only refers to MongoDB
questions.explanation: 'NoSQL' is an umbrella over at least four very different models: key-value, document, wide-column, and graph. Each is good for some access patterns and useless for others, so treating NoSQL as one thing leads to poor choices.

questions.question: What is denormalization and what tradeoff does it make?
options: Splitting data into more tables to save space; trades reads for writes
Storing data pre-joined in read-shaped form to avoid joins; trades write complexity and query flexibility for read speed and scale
Encrypting data at rest; trades CPU for security
Removing all indexes; trades correctness for speed
questions.explanation: Denormalization stores data in the shape you'll read it (e.g. embedding a user's posts inside the user document) so reads are one fast key lookup. The cost is harder writes (updating duplicated data) and loss of flexible cross-entity queries.

questions.question: For storing IoT sensor readings arriving at 1,000,000 writes per second, queried by device and time range, which model fits best?
options: Graph database
Wide-column store (e.g. Cassandra), built for write-heavy scale and key/range queries
A single relational instance
Key-value store with no range support
questions.explanation: Wide-column stores are designed for massive write volumes and queries by partition key and range — exactly the time-series/event-log pattern. A single relational instance can't absorb that write rate, and a plain key-value store can't do range queries.

questions.question: Why is 'NoSQL scales, SQL doesn't' a misleading claim?
options: It's completely true with no nuance
Modern relational databases scale enormously, and NoSQL only 'scales' for the specific access patterns it was designed for
Because SQL databases cannot store more than a gigabyte
Because NoSQL databases never need maintenance
questions.explanation: Relational databases with replicas, partitioning, and managed services scale very far, and most apps never outgrow a tuned Postgres. NoSQL scales only for its intended patterns — ask a key-value store for an ad-hoc cross-entity report and you're stuck. Choose based on which tradeoff you need.
